text: The surgeon dissects the medial attachments of the gallbladder from the dome down to the Triangle of Calot, revealing the entire medial side of the gallbladder against a backdrop of slightly fatty liver tissue.

The surgeon dissects the medial attachments of the gallbladder from the dome down to the Triangle of Calot, revealing the fatty tissue of the Triangle and providing a clear view of the medial side of the gallbladder.

The surgeon opens up the Triangle of K Low by detaching the gallbladder from the liver bed, moving from medial to lateral, which enhances the exposure of the surgical field and avoids working in a confined space.

The surgeon uses the My clip-up plier as a retractor to enhance exposure of the Triangle of Calot, adjusting it to push the liver upward as needed. The Firefly imaging system aids in the dissection, clearly showing the cystic duct connecting to the common hepatic duct to form the common bile duct.

The surgeon uses a My clip-up plier as a retractor to elevate the liver, ensuring clear exposure of the Triangle of Calot. The duodenum and abdominal fat are visible but do not obstruct the dissection area.
The Firefly imaging system enhances visualization during dissection, clearly showing the cystic duct connecting to the common hepatic duct, forming the common bile duct.
The surgeon pulls the gallbladder fundus and cystic duct away from the liver, creating an open surgical field to safely work away from critical structures like the right hepatic artery and duct.

The surgeon pulls the gallbladder fundus and cystic duct away from the liver, creating space to work safely away from critical structures like the right hepatic artery and duct. The lateral attachments of the gallbladder are being taken down before proceeding to skeletonize the cystic duct and artery.

The surgeon begins to take down the lateral attachments of the gallbladder, preparing to skeletonize the cystic duct and cystic artery. The gallbladder is visibly separated from surrounding tissues, enhancing exposure for the upcoming dissection.
The surgeon manipulates the body of the gallbladder to pull the cystic duct away from the cystic triangle, preparing to remove most of the gallbladder.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to pull the body of the gallbladder away, aiming to separate the cystic duct and artery from the hepatocystic triangle. The gallbladder is dissected towards the neck, with Firefly imaging confirming the absence of aberrant ducts joining the cystic duct.

The surgeon uses Firefly imaging to confirm the absence of aberrant ducts joining the cystic duct while dissecting towards the neck of the gallbladder, keeping the cystic duct and cystic artery distant from critical structures.

The surgeon identifies the cystic artery and uses the Firefly imaging system to ensure no aberrant ducts are connecting to the cystic duct, facilitating a careful and safe skeletonization of the cystic duct.

The surgeon uses Firefly imaging to confirm the cystic artery and ensure no other structures are joining the cystic duct. The gallbladder is fully separated from the liver, achieving the critical view of safety with only the cystic duct and artery visible entering the gallbladder.

The surgeon achieves the critical view of safety, with only the cystic duct and artery visibly entering the gallbladder. The entire gallbladder is separated from the liver, ensuring no other structures are connected to it.
The surgeon prepares the clip applier, ensuring it is ready to secure the cystic artery during the cholecystectomy procedure.

The surgeon uses a clip applier to secure a clip on the cystic artery, while the bedside assistant prepares a new clip. The cystic artery is then divided using cautery. The setup involves four ports and three instruments, providing excellent exposure without the need for scissors or additional cautery devices.

The bedside assistant replaces the clip on the clip applier. The surgeon divides the cystic artery using cautery. The surgical field is well-exposed with four ports, utilizing three instruments without the need for scissors or additional cautery devices.
The surgeon places three clips on the cystic duct, with two clips positioned on the patient's side. The cystic duct appears elongated, allowing the use of cautery in cut mode on the specimen side.
The surgeon places three clips on the cystic duct, with two clips positioned on the patient's side. The cystic duct appears elongated, allowing the use of cautery in cut mode on the specimen side.
The surgeon completes the procedure by replacing the Hassan cannula in the umbilicus with an EndoCatch bag. The gallbladder is placed into the bag and extracted through the umbilical defect.
The surgeon completes the procedure by inserting an endocatch bag through the umbilical defect to retrieve the gallbladder, which is then removed from the surgical field.
